reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: PDSZ Program 2024, 2026: commitment to reduce segregation + admission reform; documented union focus on equitable access and working conditions. S1 directly advances the documented segregation-reduction and admission-reform tracks.
reactions.interest: PDSZ member teachers in general and selective schools; equitable access to selective education; reduction of SES-skewed sorting; protection against intensified test-prep pressure on primary pupils.
reactions.public_good_frame: A fair admission system that rewards ability and effort, not family wealth and test-prep spending, improves both equity and the quality of teaching — because teachers in general schools retain high-achievers, and teaching conditions improve when classrooms are more socially balanced.
reactions.argument: S1 is compatible with the union's documented commitment to reduce segregation and reform admissions. The mechanism — replacing coachable exams with less-preparation-sensitive assessment and introducing regional equity quotas — directly weakens the SES advantage. However, the union's support hinges on three conditions: (1) the assessment redesign must not increase workload or assessment burden on teachers without compensation; (2) the SES indicator and quota implementation must not create unmanageable compliance or monitoring burden on schools; (3) the reform must be paired with sufficient advanced-programme and general-school funding so that selective-track shrinkage does not leave general schools under-resourced. The scenario assumes a workable SES proxy exists and that non-state maintainers can be brought under uniform rules — both are load-bearing, and if either fails, the reform may simply shift sorting to unregulated channels, which the union would oppose. The one-off assessment-design cost is acceptable if piloting and validation are resourced; the medium recurring compliance-audit cost is acceptable if it does not fall on schools alone.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes opposition if: (a) the assessment redesign falls to teachers to implement without training/time allocation; (b) compliance monitoring becomes an unfunded school-level burden; (c) SES proxies are gamed or impractical, allowing SES sorting to persist; (d) non-state maintainers are exempted or unregulated, causing selective demand to migrate; (e) the reform proceeds without pairing investment in general-school advanced programmes and peer support. Support becomes stronger if: (f) the reform is pilot-tested with rigorous teacher input and evaluation before national rollout; (g) assessment-design costs are fully funded through Oktatási Hivatal, not schools; (h) a statutory, monitored compliance mechanism is established with clear role boundaries (ministry vs. school).

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: PDSZ Program 2024, 2026: commitment to reduce segregation + maintain 6/8 tracks. S2 is documented as one of the union's own policy options (gradual reduction of early entry places), but the scenario's reliance on unevidenced peer-effect and family-retention links, plus the durability risk, create conditions for conditional support.
reactions.interest: PDSZ member teachers in general and selective schools; stabilization of general-school cohorts and peer composition; reduction of early sorting pressure; protection against caps being reversed or offset by non-state expansion.
reactions.public_good_frame: Gradual reduction of early selection, paired with robust general-school funding, allows more high-achievers to develop in mixed peer environments and gives teachers time to build the pedagogical capacity to teach heterogeneous classrooms. Caps protect the reform's durability if they are legislatively entrenched and applied uniformly across all maintainers.
reactions.argument: S2 aligns with the union's documented interest in reducing segregation while maintaining selective tracks. The mechanical effect of caps — shrinking the early-sorted share — is sound. However, the union's conditional support depends on: (1) the downstream peer-composition and family-retention benefits rely on unevidenced links (the scenario itself flags these as contested/weak); S2 assumes that retained high-achievers will remain in general schools and that advanced-programme funding will keep ambitious families there, but neither is demonstrated; (2) the union will not support caps that are simply offset by non-state maintainer expansion — the scenario explicitly flags this as an unresolved assumption; if caps are applied only to state schools, they fail. (3) The felmenő rendszer guarantee is sound, but the scenario calls for an 'entrenchment mechanism' (supermajority/multi-year notice) to block reversal — this is politically fragile and offers no real protection against a determined successor government (Polish precedent confirms this). (4) Advanced-programme funding must scale as caps tighten; the scenario calls this medium and formula-based, but provides no baseline or ramp trajectory. The union will not support a cap that shrinks selective seats without matching investment in general schools. (5) The union's core concern: if caps reduce selective seats without simultaneous admission reform (S1), residual selective places become more SES-skewed, harming low-SES applicants. S2 alone does not solve segregation; it may entrench it unless paired with S1.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes opposition if: (a) caps apply only to state-maintained schools and non-state maintainers are allowed to expand selective intake, nullifying the segregation-reduction goal; (b) advanced-programme funding in general schools is not credibly ramped alongside caps, leaving general schools under-resourced for the retained cohorts they absorb; (c) no admission-reform mechanism (S1-type rule change) is paired with caps, so shrinking selective seats simply increase SES skew among the residual applicants; (d) the entrenchment mechanism is weak or absent, making reversal politically cheap (Polish precedent); (e) the peer-effect benefit is assumed without pilot evidence. Support becomes stronger if: (f) caps are applied uniformly to all maintainers (state, church, foundation) via legislation or conditional subsidy; (g) advanced-programme funding is explicitly ramped on a multi-year schedule linked to the cap trajectory, with teacher training in differentiated instruction; (h) S2 is bundled with S1 (admission reform); (i) pilot evaluation in one or two districts over 2-3 admission cycles measures actual peer-composition and family-retention effects before national rollout; (j) an entrenchment mechanism is designed with real political teeth (e.g. a cross-party pact or constitutional anchor, not merely a multi-year notice).

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: PDSZ Program 2024, 2026: commitment to reduce segregation. S3 (comprehensive school to 14) is the structural reform option most directly aligned with segregation reduction, but the union's documented positions imply serious reservations about implementation risk and middle-class flight.
reactions.interest: PDSZ member teachers in general and selective schools; elimination of early sorting at ages 10–12; creation of longer, more stable mixed-ability cohorts; teacher training and working-condition support for heterogeneous-classroom pedagogy; protection against middle-class exit and sorting migration.
reactions.public_good_frame: Delaying selection to 14 allows all pupils to develop in mixed peer environments and gives teachers time to diagnose ability and potential fairly. Research from Finland and Poland shows that delayed selection, paired with rigorous teacher training and diagnostic support, reduces the SES-achievement gradient. This benefits both equity and teaching quality — teachers in de-tracked classrooms benefit from professional development, smaller class sizes, and diagnostic tools, not merely from structural change alone.
reactions.argument: S3 is the most direct structural route to segregation reduction and is documented in PDSZ's program as a policy direction. The union recognizes the moderate evidence base for delayed selection improving equity outcomes (Pekkarinen et al. 2009; Jakubowski et al. 2016). However, the union's support is heavily conditional because: (1) The scenario's own uncertainties flag the largest risk: middle-class exit to non-state and private schools. Sorting does not disappear; it relocates. The assumption that 'sorting does not simply migrate' is load-bearing and unevidenced. If 40% of urban middle-class families exit to private/church schools during the transition, general schools become even more segregated by family resources, concentrating low-SES pupils and intensifying pedagogical demand precisely where teacher shortages are acute. This harm to the low-SES majority outweighs aggregate gradient narrowing. (2) The reform bundle is non-negotiable: S3 requires simultaneous investment in teacher differentiation training, diagnostic systems (Finnish-style), and small-group remedial support. The scenario correctly identifies these as load-bearing ('equity gains require bundling; delay alone is insufficient'). Without them, heterogeneous classrooms degrade academic quality and feed the flight risk. The one-off teacher retraining cost is flagged as 'high' with no unit cost; the recurring diagnostic-support cost is 'medium-high' with no baseline. The union will not support S3 without fully-costed, credible implementation of the bundle. (3) Teacher supply is a binding constraint. The scenario flags 'acute shortage' as a known constraint; the implementation_planning section acknowledges this explicitly. Hungary's teacher shortage in maths and science is structural and long-term. De-tracked heterogeneous classrooms require more-skilled, more-experienced teachers, not fewer resources. If S3 proceeds without solving teacher shortage (via competitive salaries, working-condition improvements, and training), it will degrade the teaching environment for union members and educational quality for pupils — the union will oppose this. (4) The cross-party pact is fragile. S3 generates the 'highest-intensity opposition' from middle-class parents and prestige schools; the scenario's own political-risk section concedes that 'fallback if the pact fails is a reversible, staged pilot rather than a legislated national mandate.' If the reform lacks legislative entrenchment and a robust cross-party pact, it is vulnerable to reversal (Polish precedent is explicit). A pilot that is then reversed leaves teachers stranded with retraining they conducted for a reform that no longer exists. (5) The union's core equity concern: S3 achieves aggregate gradient narrowing only if sorting does not migrate and if teacher-intensive bundled support is actually delivered. Both are assumed, neither is assured. Pilot evaluation is essential before scaling.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes opposition if: (a) the reform proceeds without credible, fully-funded implementation of the teacher-training, diagnostic-support, and remedial-resource bundle; (b) non-state maintainer expansion is not regulated, allowing middle-class exit to relocate sorting; (c) teacher supply is not addressed (salary increases, working-condition improvements, training-uptake incentives) — attempting S3 under teacher shortage will degrade quality and harm union members; (d) the cross-party pact fails or is weak, leaving the reform vulnerable to reversal; (e) pilot evaluation is skipped or is not rigorous enough to measure sorting migration and peer-composition effects. Support becomes strong if: (f) the reform is pilot-tested in 2–3 diverse districts (urban, rural) with rigorous evaluation over at least 2–3 cohorts, measuring peer composition, sorting migration, family retention, and teacher workload/satisfaction; (g) teacher training in differentiation is sequenced before the pilot (year 1–2) and is fully compensated; (h) diagnostic systems and remedial-support funding are explicitly costed and committed, not deferred to later phases; (i) non-state maintainers are brought under uniform rules via legislation or conditional subsidy; (j) a cross-party pact with defined fallback (staged reversal if agreed thresholds are not met) is legislated; (k) salary and working-condition improvements for teachers in de-tracked environments are bundled with the reform.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: PDSZ Program 2024, 2026: commitment to reduce segregation. S4 explicitly rejects structural reform and relies on compensation to offset (not dismantle) the sorting mechanism. The union's documented commitment to reduce segregation is incompatible with a strategy that leaves the sorting engine intact.
reactions.interest: PDSZ member teachers in general and selective schools; reduction of segregation (not merely compensation for it); equitable access to high-quality education; protection against the permanent entrenchment of early selection.
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation without structural reform legitimises and entrenches the very sorting mechanism that creates inequity. Teachers in disadvantaged general schools benefit from targeted support only in the short term; if early selection remains intact, disadvantaged schools will continue to lose high-achievers and become more segregated over time. True equity improvement requires dismantling the sorting mechanism, not masking its effects with subsidies that are vulnerable to fiscal cuts.
reactions.argument: The union opposes S4 because it contradicts the documented PDSZ commitment to reduce segregation. S4 proposes to leave the 6- and 8-year track structure unchanged — the primary driver of early sorting in Hungary — and rely on targeted funding and standards to compensate for the inequitable outcomes sorting produces. This strategy fails for five reasons: (1) **Compensation does not offset, it masks segregation.** The scenario itself concedes: 'no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age.' Portugal's TEIP package improved outcomes in general schools, but Portugal did not sort pupils at ages 10–12. Applying the Portuguese playbook to Hungary's early-selection context is a category error. Compensation can narrow the outcome gradient at the margin, but it cannot offset the mechanism that created the gradient in the first place. Teachers in disadvantaged general schools will face permanently larger peer-composition challenges and lower peer-quality effects, harming working conditions and limiting pedagogical efficacy. (2) **Budget vulnerability is a fatal flaw.** The scenario explicitly flags that compensation lines are 'the first cut in fiscal consolidation' and that gains are 'reversible across fiscal cycles.' The union has watched targeted education funding eliminated or slashed in every fiscal consolidation since 2010. A strategy that depends on annual budget renewal is not a strategy; it is a hope. S4 offers no protection against cuts, whereas structural reforms (S1, S2, S3) create durable institutional changes. The union will not accept a reform whose success depends entirely on political goodwill and fiscal cycles. (3) **Entrenchment of early selection.** S4 carries a dynamic equity cost: it risks legitimising early selection by showing that 'we can compensate for it.' This sets a political precedent that sorting is acceptable as long as the losers are subsidised. Over time, compensation budgets shrink and political attention fades, but the sorting structure remains, now politically entrenched. Teachers who support early-selection abolition are undercut by a compensation strategy that makes sorting seem compatible with equity. (4) **Teacher shortage is not addressed.** S4 requires 'teacher premia for disadvantaged schools' and 'standards/support packages' but does not solve the acute shortage of maths and science teachers in disadvantaged regions. Premia help at the margin, but they do not create teachers. If disadvantaged schools cannot recruit and retain qualified teachers, compensation funding is wasted. S4 does not address this constraint; S3 (with its focus on training and working-condition improvement) at least attempts to. (5) **The union's interest in segregation reduction is abandoned.** The documented PDSZ program commits to reduce segregation through structural reform (S1, S2, S3 tracks). S4 explicitly rejects structure and proposes indefinite compensation. This is incompatible with the union's public mandate and member interests. Teachers in disadvantaged general schools benefit from targeted support, but not in perpetuity under worse peer conditions than their colleagues in selective schools. The union opposes a strategy that segregates by school while subsidising the segregated.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition becomes conditional support only if: (a) S4 is explicitly paired with a structural admission-reform component (S1-type rule change) that reduces SES skew in selective-track intake, so that compensation is paired with the beginning of sorting-mechanism reform rather than indefinite perpetuation; (b) the compensation budget is legislatively entrenched and protected against fiscal-cycle cuts (e.g. a constitutional or multi-year mandatory-funding commitment with a supermajority override requirement for cuts) and independently evaluated annually; (c) teacher supply is addressed through salary and working-condition improvements that actually shift recruitment/retention in disadvantaged schools (not merely premia on existing shortage); (d) the reform includes robust pilot evaluation in disadvantaged-school cohorts, measured against a comparison group, over at least 5 years, with clear success metrics and a defined fallback if metrics are not met. Opposition remains firm if S4 is presented as a stand-alone alternative to structural reform.
